questions.question: Define a binary tree. Differentiate between a full binary tree, a complete binary tree, and a skewed binary tree with examples.
keyPoints: Binary tree: each node has at most 2 children
Full binary tree: every node has 0 or 2 children
Complete binary tree: all levels filled except possibly last, filled left to right
Skewed binary tree: all nodes have only one child (left-skewed or right-skewed)
Examples and diagrams for each type

questions.question: Explain the process of deleting a node from a binary search tree for all three cases (leaf, one child, two children).
keyPoints: Case 1 (leaf): simply remove the node
Case 2 (one child): replace node with its child
Case 3 (two children): find inorder successor or predecessor
Replace node value with successor value and delete successor
Successor has at most one child, so reduces to case 1 or 2
Time complexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

questions.question: Derive the properties of a binary tree: maximum nodes at level k, maximum total nodes for height h, and the relationship between leaf nodes and internal nodes.
keyPoints: Max nodes at level k = 2^k
Max total nodes for height h = 2^(h+1) - 1
In a full binary tree: leaves = internal nodes + 1
Minimum height for n nodes = floor(log2(n))

questions.question: Explain array and linked representations of a binary tree. When is each preferred?
keyPoints: Array: index formulas for parent/child, good for complete trees
Linked: node struct with data, left, right pointers
Array wastes space for skewed trees
Linked representation is more flexible but uses extra pointer memory
Array preferred for heaps, linked preferred for general BSTs

questions.question: Write C functions to insert a node into and search for a node in a binary search tree.
keyPoints: Insert: compare with root, go left if smaller, right if larger
Insert at the appropriate NULL position
Search: compare with root, recurse left or right
Both operations: O(h) time complexity
Handle empty tree edge case
